comments: I already have a recent master's in Writing Studies and Educational Psychology with a 4.0 GPA, as well as over a decade of social work experience, and several academic publications, conference presentations, and workshops that focused on therapeutic techniques, mental wellbeing, or disability. I also taught for my entire master's degree, was an Assistant Director of the Writing Center, and conducted my own 100+ participant study. I was waitlisted on March 30th, told all decisions would be final by April 24th, and then received an email offering me admission into the part-time and non-funded 3-year program (rather than the full-time, funded 2-year program I had applied for) on April 29th. I was told within the next week I would get my official offer of admission, and I had that same one week to accept it and pay the $250 admission fee. I emailed back 6 days later saying I had discussed with my support network and had decided to reject the offer due to national instability and uncertainty of gov financial aid, but would like to reapply in the future. I asked how to indicate this acceptance on a potential future application, as I had never received an official admission offer. Two weeks after that, I got my official admission offer and a host of emails telling me what my next steps were. All of them had inconsistent and contradictory information, including dates that were different email to email and deadlines that had passed weeks ago. One email had three different dates for the same (important) deadline. I finally got a response to the email I had sent directly to the Admissions Office, saying they were sorry to hear I was declining and informing me of the steps I needed to take to officially decline. They did not answer the other questions I had included in my email to them. Throughout all of this, my name was only spelled correctly once. We will see if I do end up reapplying in future years.

Early Nov 2025 contacted potential supervisor and was encouraged to apply, end of Nov 2025 submitted application, mid Jan 2026 submitted application for L4E fellowship. Early Feb 2026 I get a Whatsapp message from my proposed secondary supervisor on a Sunday afternoon (Montreal time) asking if I'm available in the next couple of hours to discuss my application. As an international applicant in a different time zone unfortunately I was already asleep so replied first thing on Monday morning trying to arrange a time we could talk, and was told they would get back to me with a different time. The next thing I hear is on the 21st April when I get sent an email to look at the application portal where I find a generic rejection message. In the mean time I sent numerous follow up messages asking if they still wanted to talk with me but I never got a reply. McGill's website suggested that most applicants would hear back at the end of February and throughout March so towards the end of March I began to reach out by email to the proposed primary supervisor, program administrator, and L4E to see if they could give me any insight into the progress of my application or when I could expect to hear back. These emails and subsequent follow ups were also all completely ignored. I am obviously gutted about my application being rejected however given the seeming distinct lack of compassion, communication, and organisation shown by the anthropology department at McGill and the L4E it raises some major red flags to me about the quality of teaching and support that is offered by both the program and the fellowship. I would be very interested to hear from successful applicants about if this is the case? If the other applications I am still waiting on are also unsuccessful I will be applying for PhD programs again next year, however given the inadequacy of my experience this year I shan't be reapplying to McGill. Very disappointed.

I had a very disappointing experience applying to the PhD program in EECS at the University of Wyoming. I submitted my application in January and never received any clear communication regarding its completeness. There were no notifications about missing documents, and every time I reached out for a status update, I received vague or delayed responses. In several instances, there was no reply at all. The lack of professionalism from the department, including the Graduate Coordinator, was frustrating. As an applicant investing significant time, effort, and money into the process, I expected at least basic transparency and communication. Unfortunately, that standard was not met. What makes this worse is that the final rejection cited multiple concerns, but none of these were ever communicated earlier in the process, nor was there any opportunity to clarify or strengthen the application. It raises serious questions about how thoroughly applications are handled and whether they are reviewed in a timely and organized manner. Overall, the experience felt disorganized, dismissive, and disrespectful toward applicants. Based on this experience, I would strongly caution prospective students to think carefully before applying here. Applicants deserve timely communication, clear guidance, and professional treatment—none of which I experienced in this process.

Canadian domestic student. Applied in early March; notified by the end of March. Also note: "The University of Western Ontario" is also known as "Western University." Additional notes: I already went to graduate school here (finished with an MSc in Industrial-Organizational Psychology in the past). I completed some additional coursework (enough to warrant a second bachelor's degree in Math) and this is what the 3.60 (standardized GPA that takes into account institution) is based on. My grade average (as calculated by the school) was an 84%, somehow (I'm not sure how they arrived at this number; I think that even including only my last two years would have resulted in an average of 83.5% so maybe they rounded?). Every course on this transcript was a math, statistics, or computer science course (only one course from computer science; everything else was a roughly even split of math or statistics). I also had three summer research internships in Math and Statistics. I did not include any GRE scores because I did not include these in my application, but if anyone's curious, my old scores (back when I got into grad school the first time) were 94th percentile analytical writing, 97th percentile verbal, and 89th percentile quantitative (and I think that's because during that year, if you got perfect, it would still only be 94th percentile because of how many people would get perfect on that section). I also had prior experience with my selected supervisor (i.e., I attended his class and worked with him for one of my summer research internships).
The school won't tell you if you are rejected. I reached out to my PI directly via email a couple months after the interview and they let me know. Don't spend forever waiting and email to confirm.
